verdict_reasoning: LYN is a Src-family tyrosine kinase that is primarily cytoplasmic/inner-leaflet anchored via N-terminal lipidation (myristoylation and palmitoylation on cysteine residues). However, LYN has documented surface pools under specific conditions: (1) B-cell activation induces rapid LYN translocation to the plasma membrane as part of BCR signaling complex assembly; (2) in some immune cells, LYN cycles between cytoplasmic and membrane-proximal pools via regulated phosphorylation and lipid-binding dynamics; (3) LYN is reported in lipid-raft microdomains at the PM during T-cell and B-cell activation. The baseline state is cytoplasmic, but the activation-induced surface display and documented PM trafficking cycling qualify this as contextual rather than no.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Uncertainty over whether LYN's documented BCR/TCR-activation-induced PM translocation represents a stable enough surface pool to count as targetable by an extracellular binder, or whether the transient nature of activation-driven cycling limits accessibility. Family-lineage Src-family kinases vary in their PM-pool stability; LYN-specific surface-proteomics or live-cell surface-biotinylation data in activated B/T cells would clarify.
